benefits.eyes: Vitamin E protects eyes from age-related degeneration and oxidative stress
benefits.bones: High calcium, magnesium, and phosphorus content strengthens bone structure
benefits.skin: Vitamin E and monounsaturated fats promote skin elasticity and repair
benefits.heart: Monounsaturated fats and polyphenols reduce LDL cholesterol and inflammation
benefits.brain: Riboflavin and L-carnitine support brain energy metabolism and neurotransmitter production
benefits.digestion: High fibre content aids digestive health and promotes beneficial gut bacteria
benefits.immunity: Vitamin E and zinc activate immune cells and enhance pathogen response
benefits.blood: Iron and copper support red blood cell formation; magnesium aids blood pressure regulation

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Eat raw as a snack for maximum enzyme and nutrient content
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Dry roasted enhances flavour without adding oil
best_ways_to_eat.soaked: Soaking overnight reduces phytic acid and improves nutrient absorption
best_ways_to_eat.nut_butter: Almond butter provides concentrated protein and healthy fats
best_ways_to_eat.milk: Homemade almond milk is a dairy-free calcium-fortified alternative

interesting_facts: Almonds are the world's most produced tree nut, with California producing 80% of global supply.
It takes about 5 pounds of almonds to produce 1 pound of almond oil.
Almond extract is made from the bitter almond variety, not the sweet almonds we eat.
Almonds were the only nut mentioned in the Bible.
An almond tree can live for over 50 years and produce nuts for most of its lifespan.

benefits.eyes: Antioxidants including polyphenols and Vitamin E protect retinal cells from damage
benefits.bones: Magnesium, phosphorus, and copper are essential for bone formation and strength
benefits.skin: Omega-3 and omega-6 balance promotes skin hydration and reduces inflammation
benefits.heart: ALA (omega-3) reduces inflammation and atherosclerosis risk; lowers blood pressure
benefits.brain: DHA-like compounds and polyphenols support cognitive function and neuroprotection
benefits.digestion: Fibre feeds beneficial bacteria; improves gut microbiome diversity
benefits.immunity: Polyphenols activate immune cells; copper and zinc boost immune response
benefits.blood: Arginine supports nitric oxide production; improves blood flow and pressure

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Eat raw to preserve omega-3 content and antioxidants
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Light roasting enhances flavour without damaging healthy fats
best_ways_to_eat.oil: Walnut oil is excellent for salad dressings and light cooking
best_ways_to_eat.nut_butter: Walnut butter provides accessible protein and omega-3 fats
best_ways_to_eat.baked: Added to baked goods for texture and nutritional boost

interesting_facts: Walnuts contain ALA, an omega-3 fatty acid that the body converts to EPA and DHA (like fish oil).
The English walnut has been cultivated for over 9,000 years.
Walnut shells contain a natural dye and have been used for staining throughout history.
A handful of walnuts daily (about 28g) can significantly improve cholesterol levels.
The shape of a walnut resembles a brain, and eating walnuts genuinely benefits brain health.

benefits.eyes: Selenium and Vitamin E protect eyes from oxidative damage and age-related disease
benefits.bones: Copper and magnesium are crucial for bone collagen cross-linking and mineralisation
benefits.skin: Copper supports collagen and elastin synthesis; antioxidants prevent skin ageing
benefits.heart: Monounsaturated fats reduce LDL oxidation; copper supports heart muscle function
benefits.brain: Phosphorus and copper support neurotransmitter synthesis and myelin formation
benefits.digestion: Copper improves iron absorption and reduces digestive inflammation
benefits.immunity: Zinc and copper activate immune cells; selenium acts as an antioxidant
benefits.blood: Iron and copper form haemoglobin; manganese supports blood clotting

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Whole cashews as a snack; highest nutrient retention
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Roasted cashews develop deeper flavour; still retain nutrients
best_ways_to_eat.nut_butter: Creamy cashew butter is delicious and nutrient-dense
best_ways_to_eat.milk: Cashew milk is creamy and naturally sweet without additives
best_ways_to_eat.cooked: Added to Asian curries, stir-fries, and creamy sauces

interesting_facts: Cashew nuts grow outside the cashew apple fruit, not inside like most nuts.
The cashew shell contains toxic oils (cardol and anacardic acid) that must be removed by roasting.
Cashew nuts have a very high copper content, exceeding most other nuts.
India is the world's largest processor of cashews, despite Brazil producing more raw cashews.
Cashew nut shells are used to produce a liquid resin used in industrial applications.

benefits.eyes: Vitamin E and polyphenols protect eyes from macular degeneration
benefits.bones: Phosphorus and magnesium support bone density and structure
benefits.skin: Vitamin E, niacin, and resveratrol promote skin elasticity and repair
benefits.heart: Monounsaturated fats lower LDL; resveratrol reduces inflammation and blood clots
benefits.brain: Niacin, folate, and tyrosine support neurotransmitter production and memory
benefits.digestion: High fibre and folate feed beneficial bacteria and support colon health
benefits.immunity: Niacin and selenium boost immune cell production and antiviral defences
benefits.blood: Iron and folate prevent anaemia; magnesium regulates blood pressure

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Raw peanuts retain maximum nutritional value; roasted peanuts are more common
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Roasting enhances flavour; dry roasted is healthier than oil-roasted
best_ways_to_eat.nut_butter: Peanut butter is high in protein and satisfying
best_ways_to_eat.boiled: Boiled peanuts (common in Southern US) are soft and easier to digest
best_ways_to_eat.cooked: Added to stir-fries, curries, and Asian dishes for protein and flavour

interesting_facts: Peanuts are technically legumes, not true nuts, growing underground in seed pods.
The peanut plant flowers above ground but develops its fruit underground.
Americans consume 700 million pounds of peanut butter annually.
Peanut butter was originally developed as a protein substitute for people without teeth.
It takes about 540 peanuts to make a 12-ounce jar of peanut butter.

benefits.eyes: Lutein and zeaxanthin protect against macular degeneration and cataracts
benefits.bones: Manganese is essential for bone formation; magnesium and phosphorus support density
benefits.skin: Polyphenols and Vitamin E protect skin from sun damage and oxidative stress
benefits.heart: Plant sterols reduce cholesterol; ellagic acid reduces inflammation and atherosclerosis
benefits.brain: Manganese supports brain development; thiamine aids energy metabolism in neurons
benefits.digestion: High fibre content supports healthy digestion and colon health
benefits.immunity: Polyphenols and zinc activate immune cells; antioxidants fight pathogens
benefits.blood: Manganese and copper support haemoglobin synthesis; zinc aids red blood cell function

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Raw pecans retain maximum polyphenols and nutrients
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Roasted pecans develop richer flavour; nutrients remain relatively intact
best_ways_to_eat.candied: Pecans with natural sweeteners (though higher in sugar)
best_ways_to_eat.in_baking: Used in pecan pies, brownies, and other desserts
best_ways_to_eat.oil: Pecan oil is excellent for salad dressings and light cooking

interesting_facts: Pecans have been eaten in North America for over 9,000 years, predating the arrival of Europeans.
A single pecan tree can produce up to 100 pounds of nuts in one season.
Pecans have a higher antioxidant content than any other tree nut.
The word 'pecan' comes from the Algonquian word meaning 'nut that requires a stone to crack'.
Texas is the largest pecan-producing state in the USA.

benefits.eyes: Zeaxanthin is concentrated in pistachio kernels; protects macula from damage
benefits.bones: Phosphorus and magnesium support bone mineralisation and strength
benefits.skin: Vitamin E and proanthocyanidins protect skin from UV and oxidative damage
benefits.heart: Fibre and plant sterols lower LDL; potassium reduces blood pressure significantly
benefits.brain: B-vitamins especially B6 support neurotransmitter and myelin synthesis
benefits.digestion: High fibre content promotes digestive regularity and prebiotics for gut bacteria
benefits.immunity: Vitamin B6, E, and copper activate immune cell production and function
benefits.blood: Iron and B6 support haemoglobin production; potassium regulates blood pressure

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Raw pistachios retain highest nutrient content; naturally salty varieties
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Dry roasted enhances flavour without added oils
best_ways_to_eat.salted: Traditional roasted and salted variety is the most popular
best_ways_to_eat.as_snack: Whole pistachios require cracking, promoting slower, mindful eating
best_ways_to_eat.in_dishes: Added to salads, rice dishes, and Middle Eastern delicacies

interesting_facts: Pistachios have the highest fibre content of any tree nut.
The green colour comes from chlorophyll and is a marker of ripeness.
Pistachio trees can live for over 300 years and remain productive.
The natural opening of the pistachio shell happens as the nut matures.
Iran produces over half of the world's pistachios, followed by the United States.

benefits.eyes: Vitamin E and antioxidants protect eye tissues from oxidative damage
benefits.bones: Magnesium, manganese, and phosphorus support bone formation and density
benefits.skin: Monounsaturated fats and Vitamin E keep skin supple and protected
benefits.heart: Highest monounsaturated fat content of any nut; lowers LDL cholesterol effectively
benefits.brain: Thiamine and palmitoleic acid support brain energy and neurological health
benefits.digestion: Fibre and monounsaturated fats support healthy digestion
benefits.immunity: Selenium and copper boost immune cell production and pathogen response
benefits.blood: Copper and iron support red blood cell formation and oxygen transport

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Raw macadamia nuts are buttery and naturally delicious
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Dry roasted brings out rich, sweet flavour
best_ways_to_eat.nut_butter: Creamy macadamia nut butter is luxurious and easily digested
best_ways_to_eat.oil: Macadamia oil is excellent for salads and light cooking at high heat
best_ways_to_eat.chocolate: Macadamia nuts and dark chocolate are a classic pairing

interesting_facts: Macadamia nuts are the hardest tree nuts to crack, requiring specialised machinery.
Macadamia nuts take 7-10 years to first bear fruit after planting.
They are one of the most expensive nuts due to processing challenges.
Hawaii is the largest producer of macadamia nuts outside Australia.
The name 'macadamia' comes from John Macadam, a Scottish scientist.

benefits.eyes: Vitamin E and selenium protect eyes from oxidative degeneration
benefits.bones: Extremely high magnesium; phosphorus and copper support bone mineralisation
benefits.skin: Vitamin E and selenium protect skin from UV damage and photoageing
benefits.heart: Selenium reduces cardiovascular inflammation; magnesium regulates heartbeat
benefits.brain: Selenium is essential for thyroid hormone metabolism affecting brain function
benefits.digestion: Fibre and magnesium support healthy digestive function
benefits.immunity: Selenium is crucial for glutathione peroxidase, a master antioxidant enzyme
benefits.blood: Copper and zinc support red blood cell production and immune function

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Raw Brazil nuts retain maximum selenium and Vitamin E
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Roasting enhances flavour but should be limited to maintain selenium
best_ways_to_eat.whole: Best eaten whole to limit selenium intake to safe levels
best_ways_to_eat.in_blends: Mix with other nuts to avoid excessive selenium consumption
best_ways_to_eat.occasional: 1-2 nuts daily is the recommended amount due to high selenium

interesting_facts: Brazil nuts contain more selenium than any other food source — just 2-3 nuts meet daily needs.
Brazil nut trees can live for over 500 years and only grow in the wild.
They cannot be commercially cultivated; all Brazil nuts are wild-harvested.
The Brazil nut tree can grow up to 160 feet tall in the Amazon rainforest.
Excessive consumption of Brazil nuts can cause selenium toxicity (selenosis).

benefits.eyes: Vitamin E and zinc protect eyes from age-related degeneration
benefits.bones: High magnesium, phosphorus, and zinc support bone mineralisation and growth
benefits.skin: Vitamin E and niacin support skin barrier function and repair
benefits.heart: Monounsaturated fats reduce LDL; magnesium regulates blood pressure and heart rhythm
benefits.brain: Pantothenic acid and zinc support neurotransmitter and myelin synthesis
benefits.digestion: Zinc is essential for digestive enzyme production and gut barrier integrity
benefits.immunity: Zinc activates immune cells; copper and iron enhance immune response
benefits.blood: Iron and copper form haemoglobin; zinc maintains red blood cell integrity

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Raw pine nuts retain all nutrients; most expensive way to eat them
best_ways_to_eat.pesto: Traditional pesto ingredient; blended with basil, garlic, and olive oil
best_ways_to_eat.salads: Scattered over salads and Mediterranean dishes
best_ways_to_eat.baked: Sprinkled on baked goods for a luxurious crunch

interesting_facts: Pine nuts are the most expensive nuts due to labour-intensive harvesting from pinecones.
It takes 2-3 years for a pine cone to mature and release its nuts.
Pine nuts have been eaten for over 6,000 years, with evidence from Mediterranean civilisations.
Chinese pine nuts are cheaper but may have a lingering metallic taste (pine mouth).
Pine nuts are primarily harvested in China, Russia, and the Mediterranean region.

benefits.eyes: Vitamin E is one of the highest of any nut; protects retina from oxidative stress
benefits.bones: Manganese is essential for bone mineralisation; magnesium supports bone density
benefits.skin: High Vitamin E and manganese support skin elasticity and collagen formation
benefits.heart: Monounsaturated fats reduce LDL; copper supports heart muscle contraction
benefits.brain: Manganese, folate, and B-vitamins support cognitive function and memory
benefits.digestion: High fibre and polyphenols feed beneficial bacteria and reduce colon inflammation
benefits.immunity: Manganese activates immune enzymes; Vitamin E acts as a potent antioxidant
benefits.blood: Iron and copper support haemoglobin synthesis; folate prevents anaemia

best_ways_to_eat.raw: Raw hazelnuts have a sweet, subtle flavour
best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Roasting brings out rich, toasted flavour; darkens the kernels
best_ways_to_eat.hazelnut_butter: Smooth and delicious alternative to almond butter
best_ways_to_eat.chocolate: Traditional pairing; Nutella is hazelnut spread with chocolate
best_ways_to_eat.oil: Cold-pressed hazelnut oil for salad dressings

interesting_facts: Hazelnuts have extremely high Vitamin E content — more than 15 mg per 100g.
Turkey and Italy are the world's largest producers of hazelnuts.
Hazelnut trees have been cultivated for over 5,000 years.
The hazelnut shell opens naturally when the nut is ripe.
Hazelnuts were sacred in Celtic cultures and symbolised wisdom and protection.

benefits.eyes: Vitamin C and antioxidants protect eyes from oxidative damage
benefits.bones: Copper and manganese support bone formation and collagen cross-linking
benefits.skin: Vitamin C boosts collagen synthesis; tannins reduce skin inflammation
benefits.heart: Low fat; fibre lowers cholesterol; potassium regulates blood pressure
benefits.brain: Folate and B6 support neurotransmitter synthesis and mood regulation
benefits.digestion: Extremely high fibre — much higher than other nuts; promotes colon health
benefits.immunity: Vitamin C and tannins provide antioxidant and antimicrobial benefits
benefits.blood: Folate, copper, and iron support red blood cell production and prevent anaemia

best_ways_to_eat.roasted: Roasted chestnuts are the most traditional preparation
best_ways_to_eat.boiled: Boiling softens the inner skin for easier peeling
best_ways_to_eat.raw: Can be eaten raw but are tougher and less digestible
best_ways_to_eat.flour: Chestnut flour is gluten-free and used in baking
best_ways_to_eat.puree: Used in soups, stews, and desserts

interesting_facts: Chestnuts are unique among nuts in being very low in fat — about 2% compared to 60-75% in other nuts.
They are also much higher in carbohydrates than other nuts, making them more like grains nutritionally.
Roasted chestnuts are traditionally eaten around Christmas, especially in Mediterranean cultures.
Chestnut trees can live for several hundred years and reach great heights.
The American chestnut tree was nearly wiped out by a blight in the early 20th century.
